items.title: Constrain depth before tuning criterion
items.avoid: Grid-searching gini vs entropy while max_depth is unlimited.
items.prefer: Fix reasonable max_depth and min_samples_leaf via CV first; switch criterion only if validation differs.
items.why: Complexity limits dominate bias–variance; impurity choice is secondary for most tabular tasks.
items.example: Grid: max_depth in [3,5,7,10], min_samples_leaf in [1,5,10,20] — then compare gini vs entropy at the best depth.

items.title: Encode categoricals explicitly
items.avoid: Label-encoding unordered categories as integers 0,1,2 and letting the tree treat order as meaningful.
items.prefer: One-hot encode nominal categories (or target encoding with proper CV leakage control for high cardinality).
items.why: Ordinal splits on arbitrary integer codes impose false ordering — splits like category ≤ 1 are meaningless for nominal data.
items.example: color: red/blue/green → three one-hot columns, each splittable at 0.5.

items.title: Validate with held-out or CV metrics
items.avoid: Selecting the tree with highest training accuracy or deepest allowed depth.
items.prefer: Track cross-validated accuracy, log loss, or RMSE vs max_depth; pick simplest model near the validation minimum.
items.why: Training metrics always favor more complex trees — validation reveals overfitting.
items.example: Plot train vs val accuracy for max_depth 1..15; choose depth where val is within 1% of peak.

items.title: Use trees as interpretable baseline, not final production default
items.avoid: Deploying a single unconstrained tree because it is easy to explain, when validation lags ensembles by large margins.
items.prefer: Start with a pruned tree for EDA and stakeholder review; move to random forests or boosting when accuracy and stability matter.
items.why: Interpretability trades off variance — ensembles keep tree mechanics but stabilize predictions.
items.example: Single tree val AUC 0.78 → RandomForest val AUC 0.86 with aggregated feature importance for reporting.

items.title: Inspect rules, not just leaf accuracy
items.avoid: Reporting only top-level accuracy without reading whether leaf rules make domain sense.
items.prefer: Export tree rules (export_text, plot_tree) and sanity-check thresholds against domain knowledge.
items.why: Spurious splits on leaky or artifact features can score well on train but fail in deployment.
items.example: A split on patient_id_prefix is a leakage red flag even if train accuracy is high.
